metadata.name: Ash
metadata.title: Ash Settings
metadata.description: When laser cleaning Ash, start with controlled power levels to leverage its strong laser absorption, which removes surface contaminants efficiently without deep penetration. This property works well because Ash's open structure allows heat to spread evenly, restoring wood grain in applications like furniture refinishing or heritage pieces. Make sure you adjust scan speeds slower during initial passes, as the material's moderate density can lead to uneven heating if rushed—watch for charring on porous areas. Reduce overlap ratios to avoid overexposure, revealing clean surfaces while preserving the wood's natural flexibility for further handling.

author.name: Todd Dunning
author.title: MA
credentials: MA Optics and Photonics, UC Irvine, 2019
author.imageAlt: Todd Dunning, MA, Junior Optical Materials Specialist at Coherent Inc., with precision optics tools

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for efficient Ash removal without substrate damage

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength optimized for Ash absorption and minimal substrate interaction

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for effective Ash removal resolution and processing efficiency

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage with thermal recovery

energyDensity.description: Fluence level sufficient for Ash ablation while preserving underlying substrate

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled Ash ablation and thermal management

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform Ash removal and process throughput

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete Ash removal with process control

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage without excessive thermal buildup

power.description: Typical range for 1064 nm industrial cleaning of light powdery contaminants like ash on metals or stone; start at 200 W to balance efficiency and avoid thermal damage to low-conductivity substrates; max limited by substrate melting point (e.g., ~1000 W for steel); based on IPG Photonics and Laserax manufacturer specs for contamination removal.

scan_speed.description: Slower speeds (100-500 mm/s) for heavy ash layers to ensure complete removal, faster (1000-2000 mm/s) for light surface ash; 500 mm/s recommended for optimal energy delivery without overheating; from industry standards and equipment manuals like those from Adapt Laser Systems.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime (10-100 ns) preferred over picosecond/femtosecond for cost-effective ash cleaning due to sufficient ablation of low-thermal-conductivity particulates; 20 ns minimizes heat-affected zone (HAZ) on reflective or heat-sensitive substrates; supported by laser physics research in Optics & Laser Technology journal.

repetition_rate.description: 10-100 kHz typical for pulsed cleaning to control pulse overlap; 50 kHz recommended for ash to achieve fast processing while limiting heat accumulation in low-conductivity materials; higher rates speed up cleaning but risk substrate damage; from academic literature like studies in Applied Surface Science.

energy_density.description: 0.5-3 J/cm² range for effective vaporization/ablation of ash without exceeding substrate damage thresholds (e.g., ~4 J/cm² for metals); 1.5 J/cm² safe and recommended for powdery ash with high reflectivity at 1064 nm; based on ablation threshold studies in Journal of Laser Applications; conservative for uncertain ash composition.

spot_size.description: 100-500 μm for precise control in industrial applications; 200 μm recommended for ash removal on detailed surfaces to balance fluence and coverage; smaller for fine details, larger for broad areas; from equipment specifications by manufacturers like Coherent Inc.

pass_count.description: 1 pass for light ash contamination, up to 5 for heavy buildup; 2 passes recommended for complete, uniform removal of ash while minimizing risk to underlying substrate; drawn from industry case studies on particulate cleaning in heritage and industrial contexts (e.g., ICOMOS guidelines adapted for laser use).

overlap_ratio.description: 30-80% ensures uniform fluence distribution; 50% recommended for ash cleaning to achieve even coverage without excessive heat buildup on low-melting-point residues; typical in process optimization studies from Lasers in Engineering journal.
